level.honesty_note: Potter Creek Cave (Sec.23 T34N R4W; Sinclair 1904) and Samwel Cave (Furlong 1906 / Feranec et al. 2007) sit ~5 km (~3 mi) apart on opposite sides of the McCloud River arm of Shasta Lake and are NOT connected in reality. Every connector BETWEEN the two caves in this manifest -- chiefly the single 'corridor' joining the Potter Creek main chamber (SE end, ~X115) to Samwel's Entrance (~X165) -- is an interpretive authoring convenience to stitch two independent surveys into one continuous walkable FPS level. No surveyed passage joins the caves. WITHIN each cave the room-to-room corridors follow the documented connectivity, but all global_position_ft, level_order and stream_bbox values are interpretive staging, not surveyed coordinates. Convention: 1 unit = 1 ft, +Y up; global_position_ft is each room's geometric CENTER, so floor = center.y - height/2, with the Potter Creek main-chamber lowest floor as the y=0 datum. The player SPAWNS at the great-pit lip (start_room = pcc_great_pit, level_order 4) at the head of the 42 ft rope-ladder descent; level_order 1-3 (the daylit arched entrance, vestibule and narrow passage) are the natural way in, walkable just above/behind the spawn.

rooms.name: Arched Entrance (cave mouth)
rooms.styling: A daylit limestone arch in the high bluff on the north side of Potter Creek; the only natural human entrance. Brush and pine on the hillside above; the arch frames the transition from daylight to the lamp-and-candle dark beyond. Carboniferous McCloud (blue) limestone.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.3, Pl.3; locality Sec.23 T34N R4W, ~1500 ft elev., ~800 ft above the McCloud.
provenance.documented: Existence, arched form and position of the entrance, and that it opens into a smaller chamber (Pl.3, p.3).
provenance.interpretive: All dimensions (none were surveyed), exact arch geometry, wall rugosity, vegetation, colour and lighting. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: the cave mouth in the bluff above Potter Creek (Pl.3)
communicates with the smaller (entrance) chamber

rooms.name: Vestibule (the smaller entrance chamber)
rooms.styling: A small twilight chamber just inside the arch, floored by soft yellow ossiferous (bone-bearing) earth over five and a half feet deep resting on a limestone floor that pitches steeply toward the northwest. Several small side-cavities lead off it. Last daylight; beyond, only lamp or candle.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.3 (smaller chamber), p.10 (soft yellow ossiferous earth >5.5 ft on a NW-pitching limestone floor).
provenance.documented: Existence of the smaller chamber, its role as the antechamber to the narrow passage, the >5.5 ft ossiferous-earth deposit on a NW-pitching limestone floor, and the side-cavities.
provenance.interpretive: Plan dimensions and ceiling height (unsurveyed), exact shape, speleothems, colour and lighting. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: admittance to a narrow passageway leading back toward the great pit

rooms.name: Narrow Passage to the Ladder (the entrance gallery)
rooms.styling: A dark passage requiring lamp or candle, bending to the left from the vestibule and ending abruptly at the lip of the great pit, where a convenient stalagmite pillar gives a secure anchor for the rope ladder. Floored with soft yellow ossiferous earth; bones and charcoal lie 6-18 in below the surface.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.3 (passage terminates at the margin of a great pit; stalagmite pillar anchors a rope ladder); p.10 (entrance deposits, 6-18 in bones/charcoal).
provenance.documented: Existence and left-turning route of the passage, its termination at the pit margin, the anchoring stalagmite pillar, the ossiferous-earth floor and the 6-18 in bone/charcoal basin.
provenance.interpretive: Length, width and height (unsurveyed), cross-section shape, lighting and dressing. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: passage ends at the margin of the great pit; stalagmite pillar anchors the rope ladder

rooms.name: The Great Pit (42 ft entrance descent / rope ladder)
rooms.styling: The defining vertical traverse and the player's spawn: a 42-ft sheer drop from the passage margin down onto the main-chamber floor near the NW end, descended on a rope ladder lashed to a stalagmite pillar at the lip. A cool shaft of daylight and dust motes falls down the pit (interpretive lighting); the 42 ft drop is to scale.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.3 (42-ft vertical descent, rope ladder, stalagmite pillar anchor).
provenance.documented: The 42 ft vertical descent itself, the rope-ladder access and the stalagmite-pillar anchor; its position near the NW end of the chamber.
provenance.interpretive: Pit plan diameter (unsurveyed; ~8 ft in the model), the daylight beam, dust motes and all lighting. LAYOUT: this is the start_room; global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: the pit margin at the top, with the stalagmite-pillar ladder anchor
42 ft vertical rope-ladder descent down into the great room

rooms.name: Main Chamber (the great room)
rooms.styling: One large chamber trending NW-SE, parallel to the strike of the McCloud limestone. Both walls slope west; the west wall overhangs with massive pendants. Floor is two coalescing breccia fans. Named rock masses sit on the floor: the Altar, two white-calcite Rock Benches, and a Fallen Block bearing Richardson's 1878 signature. Loose blocks litter both slopes; bat guano up to 1.5 ft deep along the east wall. The 75 ft is floor-to-chimney-reach above the LOWEST floor point; the chamber's own ceiling is only ~10-20 ft, the 75 ft reached only up the chimneys above the fan apices.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.3 (107 x ~30 x ~75, NW-SE, west overhang + pendants), Pl.1, Pl.4, Pl.14; pp.3-4 (loose blocks, bat guano).
provenance.documented: 107 ft length, ~30 ft max width, ~75 ft roof above the lowest floor point, NW-SE trend, west-wall overhang and pendants, the Altar / two Rock Benches / Fallen Block, loose-block scatter and the 1.5 ft bat-guano band along the east wall.
provenance.interpretive: Exact wall rugosity and colour, all speleothem geometry, pools, breakdown placement, the scale figure, all lighting, and the ~10-20 ft ceiling profile traced from the Pl.11 section. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry, including the cross-cave corridor to Samwel.

connections.note: the rope ladder back up the 42 ft pit to the entrance passage
the NW half of the floor -- the northwest breccia fan
the SE half of the floor -- the southeast breccia fan
opening in the WEST wall, buried beneath ~11 ft of stratified deposits (Pl.11 I / Pl.13 Fig.5 I)
small tunnel in the EAST wall, ~12 ft above the floor, opposite the Altar
a small grotto at the extreme NW end (not shown on the Pl.14 map)
INTERPRETIVE cross-cave connector to Samwel's Entrance -- the two caves are ~5 km apart in reality; this corridor is an authoring convenience, NOT survey geometry

rooms.name: Northwest Breccia Fan (floor sub-space)
rooms.styling: The fan filling the NW half of the chamber, sloping up from the coalescence to an apex beneath the NW chimney. The richly stratified end of the cave: S surface stalagmite, A clay-with-gravel to 13.5 ft, B limestone gravel, C volcanic ash (BARREN, in a standing pool), D clay with fallen blocks, E cemented breccia ('false floor'), F soft clay, G stalagmite/chocolate-mud, over H stalagmite bosses on the limestone floor. Bones occur throughout A,B,D,E,F,G -- a mixed, time-averaged assemblage, NOT depth-sorted.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 pp.3-7 (fan form + coalescence), p.5 stratigraphy A-H, p.11 (C ash and chocolate mud barren), p.19; strata_spec.json potter_creek_column.
provenance.documented: The fan landform sloping from the NW end and coalescing at mid-chamber, its apex under the NW chimney, the full A-H sequence with stratum A's 13.5 ft greatest clay depth; that C (ash) and the G chocolate-mud are barren.
provenance.interpretive: Footprint (~50 x 30 ft read off the plan), apex height, and that the cumulative A-H stack is a render composite of 'greatest' thicknesses, NOT additive measured depth. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: the two fans coalesce at their borders in the middle of the chamber
a near-vertical chute rises above the NW fan apex

rooms.name: Northwest Chimney (the chute above the NW fan apex)
rooms.styling: A near-vertical chute rising above the NW fan apex, climbed by rope-and-ladder (Pl.5). 41 ft up, a small arched cavity opens onto an earth-choked fissure toward the surface, with live pine roots in the clay fill. One of the natural conduits through which the cave earth and bones were washed in.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 pp.3-4, Pl.5 (NW chute climbed 41 ft to an arched cavity + earth-choked fissure with live pine roots).
provenance.documented: The chimney's existence above the NW apex, the 41 ft climb to an arched cavity, the earth-choked fissure toward the surface and the live pine roots.
provenance.interpretive: Shaft diameter (unsurveyed), exact profile; a natural conduit, not a human entrance. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: drops onto the NW fan apex
41 ft up: arched cavity -> earth-choked fissure with pine roots (impassably small)

rooms.name: Small Grotto at the Extreme NW End
rooms.styling: A small grotto opening off the extreme northwest end of the main chamber, beyond the NW fan apex -- noted on the plan key as present but omitted from the topographic map (Pl.14). A minor recess of the great room.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 Pl.14 explanation (topographic map of the floor of the main chamber, contour interval 6 in).
provenance.documented: Existence and location at the extreme NW end of the chamber (named in the Pl.14 key).
provenance.interpretive: All dimensions and shape (deliberately not mapped; nominal ~8 x 8 x 6 ft). LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: small recess off the extreme NW end; omitted from the Pl.14 plan

rooms.name: Buried Galleries -- Principal Gallery
rooms.styling: A buried chamber system found only during the NW-fan dig -- its mouth in the west wall lay under ~11 ft of stratified deposits (Pl.11 I / Pl.13 Fig.5 I). The principal gallery runs 42 ft parallel to the main room, floored with cave earth washed in from the great room, capped by a creamy-white stalagmite shell (3-4 in) over chocolate-coloured mud more than 3 ft thick.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 pp.8-9, Pl.11 (longitudinal section), Pl.13 Fig.5 (entrance I).
provenance.documented: The gallery's existence and 42 ft length, its NW-SE orientation parallel to the main room, the ~11 ft of deposit burying its west-wall opening, the false-floor-continuous earth floor, the stalagmite shell and the >3 ft chocolate mud beneath.
provenance.interpretive: Gallery width and height (unsurveyed; ~8 x 8 ft in the model), exact cross-section. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: buried opening in the west wall of the main chamber (under ~11 ft of deposit)
prolonged at its NW end by a low narrow tunnel
joined on the west by a semi-circular passage

rooms.name: Buried Galleries -- Low Narrow Tunnel (NW prolongation)
rooms.styling: A low, narrow continuation of the principal gallery off its NW end, 11 ft long -- the deepest NW reach of the buried system, floored by the same down-sloping cave earth hardening to breccia toward the rock floor.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.9.
provenance.documented: Existence, the 11 ft length, the low/narrow character and its position at the NW end of the principal gallery.
provenance.interpretive: Width and height (unsurveyed; described only as 'low' and 'narrow'). LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: continues the principal gallery toward the NW

rooms.name: Buried Galleries -- Semi-circular Passage
rooms.styling: A side passage with a semi-circular (arched) cross-section joining the principal gallery on its west side; its floor is bare blue McCloud limestone, with only a little earth and a few bones washed in -- a cleaner, less-filled branch than the earth-choked main gallery.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.9.
provenance.documented: Existence, its junction on the west side of the principal gallery, the semi-circular cross-section and the blue-limestone floor with minor earth/bones.
provenance.interpretive: Length, width and height (unsurveyed). LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: joins the principal gallery on the west

rooms.name: Southeast Breccia Fan (floor sub-space)
rooms.styling: The fan filling the SE half of the chamber, sloping up to an apex beneath the SE chimney. Structurally much simpler than the NW fan: one mass of clay + rock fragments cemented by stalagmite into a hard breccia (lumped as 'stratum A' on Pl.12), with soft-earth lenses. Worked by blasting a vertical face. Loose blocks especially abundant; on its surface lay a partial Arctotherium skull + Ursus remains.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.3 (fan form), p.8 (SE-fan structure, all stratum A cemented breccia), p.11 (Arctotherium skull + Ursus on the SE-fan surface).
provenance.documented: The fan landform sloping from the SE end and coalescing at mid-chamber, its apex under the SE chimney, that it is structureless cemented breccia (lumped as stratum A), and the documented Arctotherium+Ursus surface occurrence.
provenance.interpretive: Footprint extent and apex height (read from the plan/section), exact slope and surface relief. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: the two fans coalesce in the middle
a vertical chimney rises above the SE fan apex

rooms.name: Southeast Chimney (above the SE fan apex)
rooms.styling: A vertical chimney rising above the SE fan apex that branches upward into several openings too small to follow -- a partly-choked natural conduit toward the surface. A deep pocket-like hole full of bone-bearing calcareous earth leads off it.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 p.4 (SE chimney sub-dividing into openings too small to follow; the bone-bearing pocket leading off the chute).
provenance.documented: The chimney's existence above the SE apex, that it sub-divides into impassable openings toward the surface, and that a bone-bearing pocket leads off it.
provenance.interpretive: Height and diameter (unsurveyed; ~30 ft in the model), exact branching geometry. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: drops onto the SE fan apex
sub-divides upward into several openings too small to follow
a deep pocket-like bone-bearing hole leads off the chute

rooms.name: Southeast Chute Pocket (the large vertical bone tunnel)
rooms.styling: A large vertical pocket, 6-7 ft in diameter, opening off the SE chute and dropping straight down -- densely bone-bearing in a highly calcareous earthy matrix that grew more clayey with depth. The dig was carried 9 ft down and then abandoned for want of ventilation, so its base is open / undetermined.

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 pp.9-10.
provenance.documented: Existence, the 6-7 ft diameter, the vertical descent, the 9 ft worked depth (excavation ceased, base undetermined), the calcareous bone-bearing fill and the narrow vertical surface conduit.
provenance.interpretive: Full depth below 9 ft (undetermined), exact wall shape. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: opens off the chute at the SE end of the cave
a narrow vertical opening admits material toward the surface (impassable)

rooms.name: East-Wall Pocket (tunnel opposite the Altar)
rooms.styling: A small pocket in the east wall, its irregular mouth ~12 ft up the wall opposite the Altar, running downward about 15 ft into the rock. It held a little earth and poorly-preserved bones in a highly calcareous matrix (from sloughing stalagmite on the conduit walls).

provenance.citation: Sinclair 1904 pp.9-10.
provenance.documented: Existence, position in the east wall opposite the Altar, the irregular entrance ~12 ft above the floor, the ~15 ft downward run and the calcareous bone-bearing fill.
provenance.interpretive: Bore width/height (described only as 'small' / 'irregular'; ~4 x 4 ft in the model). LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: irregular mouth ~12 ft up the east wall, opposite the Altar

rooms.name: Entrance
rooms.styling: Displaced, normal-mapped PBR limestone shell opening toward the viewer. One of the two outside entrances: a layered emissive god-ray daylight shaft with dust motes plus a cool daylight PointLight. Breccia floor patch and angular breakdown blocks; no dripstone (entrances are drip-free).

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2.
provenance.documented: The Entrance is a named space in Furlong's plan (redrawn in Feranec et al. 2007 Fig. 2); the cave is a branching two-level system in the McCloud Limestone at 460 m elevation.
provenance.interpretive: Room size and ellipsoidal shape, the daylight god-ray shaft and dust motes, lighting, breccia floor and breakdown placement, all surface texture. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level; the corridor to Potter Creek is a cross-cave authoring convenience (caves ~5 km apart), not survey geometry.

connections.note: outside entrance; daylight god-ray shaft and daylight PointLight
INTERPRETIVE cross-cave connector back to Potter Creek -- the two caves are ~5 km apart in reality; this corridor is an authoring convenience, NOT survey geometry

rooms.name: Porcupine Entrance
rooms.styling: PBR limestone shell. The second outside entrance: a smaller god-ray daylight shaft with dust motes and a cool daylight PointLight; breccia floor patch and breakdown blocks; no dripstone. A parallel daylight branch converging with the main Entrance on Pleistocene Hall.

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2.
provenance.documented: Porcupine Entrance is a named space in Furlong's plan as redrawn by Feranec et al. (2007).
provenance.interpretive: Room size and shape, the daylight shaft, lighting, floor breccia and breakdown, all surface detail. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: second outside entrance; daylight god-ray shaft and PointLight

rooms.name: Pleistocene Hall
rooms.styling: Largest chamber. Displaced PBR limestone shell with flowstone stalactites hanging from the domed roof, a floor-to-roof flowstone column, breakdown blocks and a breccia floor patch. A standing pool of rippled PBR water on the floor -- one of the Cave of the Magic Pools.

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2.
provenance.documented: Pleistocene Hall is a named space in Furlong's plan (Feranec et al. 2007 Fig. 2). The cave is the 'Cave of the Magic Pools' with several standing water pools (Wintu medicine men bathed for 'magic strength').
provenance.interpretive: Room size and ellipsoidal shape, every speleothem, breakdown blocks, the exact pool placement, lighting, and surface texture. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: PASSAGES tube (139,29)->(128,24)
PASSAGES tube (163,13)->(150,17)
through the Gate -- PASSAGES tube (72,21)->(68,19), r 6

rooms.name: the Gate
rooms.styling: A short PBR-limestone tube -- the constriction linking Pleistocene Hall to Chamber One. Rendered with no speleothems; labeled on screen as 'the Gate' in a dimmer caption color to mark it as a passage rather than a chamber.

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2.
provenance.documented: the Gate is a named narrow passage in Furlong's plan as redrawn by Feranec et al. (2007), joining Pleistocene Hall to Chamber One.
provenance.interpretive: Its bore radius and length, the exact label position, and that it is rendered as a smooth tube; the survey gives no passage dimensions. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: passage mouth near (72,21)
passage mouth near (68,19)

rooms.name: Chamber One
rooms.styling: PBR limestone shell with flowstone stalactites, a flowstone column, breakdown blocks and a breccia floor patch; a standing pool of rippled PBR water on the floor. The head of the deep ~90 ft drop opens from this chamber's floor. One of the two excavated chambers (dated dig pits, 4-ft^2, 10-in levels) implied by the breccia floor.

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2; stratigraphy from Feranec et al. (2007) via docs/cave-paper-notes.md.
provenance.documented: Chamber One is a named space in Furlong's plan (Feranec et al. 2007 Fig. 2) and an excavated chamber: earth+breccia (30-140 cm) -> flowstone (2.5-10 cm) -> breccia (60 cm) -> gravel (10-45 cm) -> flowstone cap (3 cm) -> reddish clay; 4-ft^2 pits, 10-in levels, deepest dig 2.5 m.
provenance.interpretive: Room size and shape, speleothems, breakdown, exact pool placement, lighting; the dig stratigraphy rendered as a generic breccia floor. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: through the Gate, tube (72,21)->(68,19)
the deep drop to the lower level -- documented as requiring climbing gear; by legend the ~90 ft hole the Lost Maiden Olchanolmet fell down

rooms.name: Merriam's Chamber
rooms.styling: PBR limestone shell with flowstone stalactites, breakdown blocks and a breccia floor patch (no column). The innermost upper-level chamber, named for excavator John C. Merriam; lit by a warm lantern PointLight. Sits a little higher than Chamber One (a short rise up the connecting passage).

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2.
provenance.documented: Merriam's Chamber is a named space in Furlong's plan as redrawn by Feranec et al. (2007); excavation 1903-1906 by E. L. Furlong, J. C. Merriam and W. J. Sinclair.
provenance.interpretive: Room size and shape, speleothems, breakdown, lighting, and all surface detail. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

rooms.name: Chamber Two (Furlong's Room)
rooms.styling: Lower-level chamber ('Furlong's Room'), reached only by the ~90 ft drop and sitting ~96 ft below Chamber One's floor in this layout. PBR limestone shell with flowstone dripstone, breakdown blocks and a breccia floor patch; a warm lantern PointLight. On its floor sits the famous sacred Magic Pool. On-screen labels mark 'the deep drop (~90 ft, by legend)', the 'Pit of the Lost Maiden', and a blue return-portal Anchor. The deepest dig pits (to 2.5 m) are here.

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121, Fig. 2; stratigraphy from Feranec et al. (2007) via docs/cave-paper-notes.md.
provenance.documented: Chamber Two ('Furlong's Room') is a named lower-level space in Furlong's plan (Feranec et al. 2007 Fig. 2), reached only by a deep drop requiring climbing gear, and an excavated chamber sharing Chamber One's stratigraphy (deepest dig 2.5 m). By Winnemem Wintu tradition the Lost Maiden Olchanolmet fell to her death down a ~90-foot-deep hole into the lower level (legend, not survey).
provenance.interpretive: Room size and shape, the deep floor depth, identifying the documented deep drop AS the maiden's ~90 ft hole (modeled as one shaft), the exact pit geometry, speleothems, breakdown, pool placement, lighting, the legend labels and the return-portal Anchor. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: the only access -- the deep drop / shaft from Chamber One's floor; requires climbing gear (documented); by legend the ~90 ft hole the Lost Maiden fell down
the sacred Magic Pool lies on this chamber's floor
interpretive return-portal Anchor -> shasta_hub.x3d#Hub; not a real passage

rooms.name: the Magic Pool
rooms.styling: The famous sacred pool deep in the lower level: a rippled PBR water disc with an inner luminous teal glow and a soft blue-white PointLight rising from it, so it reads as the restorative pool. Treated respectfully -- labeled 'the Magic Pool' in a cool, distinct caption color; the Pit of the Lost Maiden lies just above it.

provenance.citation: Furlong, E.L. (1906) Am. J. Sci. 22:235-247; Feranec et al. (2007) Radiocarbon 49(1):117-121.
provenance.documented: Samwel is the 'Cave of the Magic Pools' with several standing water pools; Winnemem Wintu medicine men bathed in the pools to gain 'magic strength' (sacred site; the Lost Maiden Olchanolmet tradition).
provenance.interpretive: The number, size, and placement of the pools, designating one as THE Magic Pool, its luminous glow and PointLight, and all surrounding lighting and detail. LAYOUT: global_position/level_order/stream_bbox are interpretive staging for the unified walkable level, not survey geometry.

connections.note: the Magic Pool sits on the floor of the lower-level Chamber Two; the only way in is the ~90 ft drop
